meta.title: Lepik Line — Bohemian/Moravian Ancestry Research (Pre-Frank Lepik)
meta.relation_to_hunter: Frank Lepik is Hunter Spence's maternal 3rd-great-grandfather via: Frank Lepik → Fred Charles Lepick Sr. → Jennive Lepick → Leroy Mattingly → Sharyn Mattingly → Rachel Spence → Hunter Spence
meta.research_scope: Push the Lepik line back before Frank Lepik (~1862 Bohemia/Moravia). Research Frank's parents, village of origin, Czech genealogy databases, immigration patterns.

confidence_schema.probable: Strong single source or multiple compatible indirect sources
confidence_schema.possible: Circumstantial evidence; requires primary record to confirm
confidence_schema.unverified: Not yet found in any accessed source

summary.frank_lepik_status: Frank Lepik (František Lepík, ~1862–1939) is confirmed as the earliest known Lepik ancestor. He immigrated alone to the USA in 1881, settling in Brown County, Kansas. He was Roman Catholic, worked as a shoemaker, and married Mary Mikeska in 1885. His parents are UNKNOWN — WikiTree Lepik-8 explicitly lists both parents as unknown, and no US death certificate, naturalization paper, or ship manifest naming his parents has been located or directly accessed in this research pass.
summary.village_of_origin: Most probable regional origin: Frýdek-Místek District, Moravian-Silesian Region, Czech Republic (historical Austrian Empire / Austria-Hungary). Confidence: POSSIBLE with strong circumstantial support. No primary record naming a specific village has been found.
summary.parents: Frank Lepik's father and mother are UNVERIFIED. No US or Czech record accessed in this research pass names them. The single highest-priority step to break this wall is ordering Frank Lepick's 1939 Kansas death certificate ($15, Kansas Office of Vital Statistics).
summary.czech_line_depth: Czech Catholic parish records in the Frýdek-Místek area run from approximately 1630. If Frank's village of origin is confirmed, records could theoretically trace the Lepík line 5–7 generations before Frank (back to ~1680–1750). Currently no Czech records have been directly accessed.
summary.related_czech_texas_lepik: A parallel Lepik family exists in Texas (Jaromir Lepik, father of Emilie Lepik Zahradnik, b. 1925 in Moravia, Texas). This Lepik family settled in Austin County / Wharton County, Texas. Their connection to Frank Lepik of Kansas is UNVERIFIED — they may share Czech-Moravian origins, but no documentary link between the two Lepik lines has been found.
summary.key_finding: Chain migration evidence strongly places Frank Lepik's origins in the Frýdek-Místek District: (1) The Lepík surname has 88 total Czech bearers with 54.5% in Frýdek-Místek municipality; (2) Frank's wife Mary Mikeska descended from Josef Mikeska of Moravia; (3) The Hrenchir (Hrncir) family from Sviadnov, Frýdek-Místek District also settled in Everest, Brown County, Kansas — the same town and Catholic church where Frank Lepik married; (4) Frydek, Texas was named by Czech immigrants from the Frýdek-Místek area, confirming this region as a documented emigration source. All four lines of evidence independently point to the same 30km Moravian-Silesian corridor.

confirmed_ancestors.name: Frank Lepik
confirmed_ancestors.birth_place: Czech lands under Austria-Hungary (census: 'Bohemia, Austria' — generic label covering Bohemia, Moravia, and Czech Silesia)
confirmed_ancestors.buried: Kennekuk Cemetery, Horton, Brown County, Kansas
confirmed_ancestors.fact: Immigrant ancestor. Arrived USA 1881 alone, age ~19. Settled Brown County, Kansas. Roman Catholic. Shoemaker/boot maker in Horton, Kansas. Member I.O.O.F. Lodge No. 331. Married Mary Mikeska 13 January 1885, St. Joseph Catholic Church, Everest, Kansas. Nine children. Briefly relocated to Hazen, Arkansas (~1900–01) before returning to Kansas.
confirmed_ancestors.spouse: Mary Mikeska (WikiTree Mikeska-78, b. ~1863 Bohemia/Moravia, d. ~1951 Kansas)
confirmed_ancestors.parents: UNKNOWN — both parents unknown per WikiTree Lepik-8; no US death certificate, naturalization paper, or ship manifest naming parents has been accessed

sources.notes: Immigration year recorded as 1881

sources.collection: Kansas County Marriages, Brown County, Book D
sources.place: St. Joseph Catholic Church, Everest, Kansas

confirmed_ancestors.name: Mary Mikeska
confirmed_ancestors.buried: Kennekuk Cemetery, Horton, Brown County, Kansas
confirmed_ancestors.fact: Frank Lepik's wife. Arrived USA 1871 with her parents Josef and Johana Mikeska, age ~8. Roman Catholic. Her father Josef Mikeska is confirmed as Moravian-born (b. 1830 Moravia, d. 1902 Brown County Kansas).
confirmed_ancestors.parents: Josef Mikeska (WikiTree Mikeska-77) and Johana (maiden surname unknown) Mikeska

confirmed_ancestors.name: Josef Mikeska
confirmed_ancestors.birth_place: Moravia, Austria-Hungary (specifically: likely Zádveřice area, Holešov District, based on family connection to WikiTree Mikeska-20)
confirmed_ancestors.death_place: Horton, Brown County, Kansas, United States
confirmed_ancestors.buried: Kennekuk Cemetery, Horton, Brown County, Kansas
confirmed_ancestors.fact: Mary Mikeska's father and Frank Lepik's father-in-law. Moravian immigrant, arrived USA 1871 with wife and children. Farmer in Brown County, Kansas. His death was noted in the Horton Commercial and Kansas Democrat. His wife Johana's obituary (Everest Enterprise 1908) names their children: Frank, Mary, and Vincent Mikeska.
confirmed_ancestors.parents: UNKNOWN — WikiTree Mikeska-77 lists both parents as unknown

probable_ancestors.name: Johana Mikeska (maiden surname unknown)
probable_ancestors.birth_place: Bohemia/Moravia, Austria-Hungary (probable, by analogy with husband Josef)
probable_ancestors.fact: Wife of Josef Mikeska, mother of Mary Mikeska Lepick. Documented in Everest Enterprise obituary 1908 which names her marriage to Josef ~1859 in Czech lands and her three children.

probable_ancestors.name: Petr Mikeska
probable_ancestors.birth_place: Zádveřice, Holešov, Moravia (present-day Zlín Region, Czech Republic)
probable_ancestors.fact: Mill operator in Zádveřice, Moravia. Documented via FamilySearch entry modified 2019 and WikiTree Mikeska-20. WikiTree Mikeska-20's 'Explore More' includes Mary (Mikeska) Lepick (1863–1951), suggesting he is a relative of Josef Mikeska. Exact relationship to Josef Mikeska (1830) is possible grandfather or great-uncle — not directly confirmed. The Zádveřice Mikeska connection is corroborated by the scholarly book 'Emigrants from the Southeastern Moravia, Particularly from Zádveřice and Its Surrounding Area to Texas, 1850-1915' by Jan Mikeska (2003).
probable_ancestors.spouse: Anna Silar Mikeska (b. 1810, Horní Čermná, Lanškroun, Cechy)
probable_ancestors.notes: Petr's connection to Josef Mikeska (1830–1902, the confirmed grandfather via Mary Mikeska Lepick) is probable but not directly documented. The same Mikeska family from Zádveřice produced emigrants to Kansas AND Texas, corroborating the chain migration network.

Czech Catholic parish records in the Frýdek-Místek area cover this birth period and could confirm his name once the village is known.

unverified_leads.name: František Lepík I (Geni profile 6000000191966244826)
unverified_leads.status: possible — unverified connection to Frank Lepik
unverified_leads.fact: Geni.com has a profile 'František Lepík I' with parents listed as 'Cyril Lepík and Rosina Lepíková' and children including 'Anežka Bílková, Johanna Lepíková, and Antonín Lepík'. This is a Czech-based profile. The Geni profile content was not directly accessible during this research (page returned no extractable content), but the data was surface-indexed by search engines. If this is a real documented individual from the Frýdek-Místek area, the parents 'Cyril Lepík' and 'Rosina Lepíková' would represent the generation before Frank Lepik. CRITICAL CAVEAT: Geni collaborative trees often contain unverified user-contributed data. This lead requires verification against Czech parish records before accepting.

unverified_leads.name: Tomáš Lepík and Terezie Lepíková
unverified_leads.fact: Geni.com has a second profile 'František Lepík (1888-1888)' with parents listed as 'Tomáš Lepík and Terezie Lepíková'. This child died in 1888, which suggests parents alive in the 1880s. This Tomáš Lepík could represent an uncle or cousin of Frank Lepik born ~1862. Location unknown from search results.

regional_origin_analysis.working_hypothesis: Frýdek-Místek District, Moravian-Silesian Region, Czech Republic
regional_origin_analysis.confidence_note: Four independent lines of circumstantial evidence converge on this same 30km corridor. No primary record (death certificate, naturalization paper, ship manifest, or Czech parish record) has been directly accessed naming Frank's village. The hypothesis is well-supported but requires a primary record to confirm.

evidence.detail: Forebears.io confirms Lepík (Czech-accented variant) has exactly 88 bearers worldwide, all in Czech Republic. KdeJsme.cz 2016 data shows 54.5% reside in Frýdek-Místek municipality, 12.5% in Frýdlant nad Ostravicí (8km south), 14.8% in Ostrava — approximately 82% within a 30km radius. This extreme concentration for a surname with only 88 Czech bearers indicates single-family geographic origin in the Frýdek-Místek district.

The Hrenchir (Hrncir) family from Sviadnov, Moravia (today in Frýdek-Místek District) settled in Everest, Brown County, Kansas — the EXACT SAME TOWN where Frank Lepik married Mary Mikeska at St. Joseph Catholic Church in 1885. Joseph Valentine Hrnchir (b. 1847 Sviadnov) is documented with descendants in Brown County Kansas. This is direct evidence that Frýdek-Místek District Moravians formed a chain migration community in Everest, Kansas. The Hrenchir family's presence at the same church and town as Frank Lepik is the strongest single piece of circumstantial evidence for Frank's regional origin.
evidence.source: Find a Grave memorial 92933027 (Joseph Valentine Hrnchir); CGSI publication: Joan Hrenchir, 'The Hrenchir Family and Other Moravians of Brown County, Kansas' (1997)

evidence.detail: Mary Mikeska's father Josef Mikeska is confirmed Moravian-born (1830 Moravia). The Mikeska family traces to Zádveřice, Holešov, Moravia (WikiTree Mikeska-20, Petr Mikeska 1793–1864). The scholarly work 'Emigrants from the Southeastern Moravia, Particularly from Zádveřice and Its Surrounding Area to Texas, 1850-1915' by Jan Mikeska (2003) documents that among Austin County Texas Czech settlers, 'many were from Zadverice; but Vsetin, Frydek-Mistek, Jemnice, Chotebudice and other areas were also well represented.' This confirms Frýdek-Místek as a documented emigration source sending immigrants to the same Czech-American network as the Mikeska family.

The Texas community of Frydek (Austin County, established ~1895) was 'apparently named after the Moravian city of Frýdek-Místek' (Texas State Historical Association Handbook of Texas). Czech immigrants from the Frýdek-Místek area settled both in Central Texas AND in Brown County, Kansas, creating parallel migration corridors. A separate Texas Lepik family (Jaromir Lepik, father of Emilie Lepik Zahradnik b. 1925 in Moravia, TX) may represent another branch of the same Moravian Lepík family.

FamilySearch documents that 'immigrants from Moravia often immigrated through Galveston, Texas instead of Eastern ports and many settled in Texas' while 'immigrants from Bohemia generally immigrated through New York or other Eastern ports and initially settled in the Midwest.' Frank Lepik went to Kansas (Midwest pattern, pre-1906 Eastern port). His wife Mary Mikeska's family, confirmed Moravian, also came to Kansas, suggesting some Moravians did go north. The Kansas/Brown County Czech community drew from both Bohemia proper and Moravia-Silesia.

candidate_villages.note: Documented origin village of the Hrenchir (Hrncir) family who settled in Everest, Brown County, Kansas — same community as Frank Lepik. Sviadnov is 13km south of Ostrava, urbanistically fused with Frýdek-Místek. First written mention 1267. Catholic parish records available through Opava Archive.
candidate_villages.confidence: possible — strongest candidate by chain migration evidence

candidate_villages.note: Holds 54.5% of all Czech Lepík surname bearers. Historically divided: Místek was in Moravia; Frýdek in Czech Silesia. Catholic parish records from ~1630.
candidate_villages.confidence: possible — strongest candidate by surname distribution

candidate_villages.note: 8km south of Frýdek-Místek. 12.5% of Czech Lepík bearers. Catholic parish records from 1695.

czech_texas_lepik_parallel.subject: Jaromir Lepik family, Moravia, Texas (collateral Czech Lepik line)
czech_texas_lepik_parallel.status: separate family — possible distant cousins, no documentary connection to Frank Lepik of Kansas found
facts: Jaromir Lepik (birth year unknown) married Alzbeta Orsak Lepik. They lived in Moravia, Texas (Lavaca County), a Czech community named after the region in Central Europe.
Their daughter Emilie Lepik Zahradnik was born October 3, 1925 in Moravia, Texas. She died May 14, 2016.
Siblings: Jerome Lepik (predeceased, married Joyce), Marie Lepik Kosik (predeceased, married John), Lillie Lepik Talafuse (living 2016, Rosenberg TX).
The family moved to a farm outside Hungerford, Wharton County, Texas ~1928.
The Texas Czech Lepik family immigrated via the Galveston/Moravian route (not the Kansas/New York route), consistent with Moravian origin.

Given the extreme rarity of the Lepík surname (88 Czech bearers total), the Jaromir Lepik Texas family and Frank Lepik Kansas family almost certainly share common Czech ancestors, but the specific connection is unverified.
Moravia, Texas is in Lavaca County — one of the primary Czech Texan settlement counties (Fayette, Austin, Lavaca).

czech_immigration_context.bohemia_kansas_pattern: Czech immigrants from Bohemia/Moravia/Silesia going to Kansas generally came via New York or Baltimore, then west by rail. Brown County, Kansas was settled by Czech Catholics in the 1860s–1880s. The Everest/Horton area had at least one documented Frýdek-Místek District community (Sviadnov-origin Hrenchir family at the same Catholic church as Frank Lepik).
czech_immigration_context.moravia_texas_pattern: Moravian immigrants overwhelmingly came via Galveston, Texas. The majority of Czech Texans are Moravian. Fayette, Austin, Lavaca counties were the primary settlement areas. Texas community names — Frydek, Moravia, Vsetin, Bila Hora — directly reflect Moravian village origins.

key_book_for_mikeska_line.title: Emigrants from the Southeastern Moravia, Particularly from Zádveřice and Its Surrounding Area to Texas, 1850-1915
key_book_for_mikeska_line.author: Jan Mikeska
key_book_for_mikeska_line.relevance: Documents migration from Zádveřice, Holešov, Moravia — proven origin of the Mikeska family (Frank Lepik's wife's family). Also confirms Frýdek-Místek as a documented source for the same migration network.

key_book_for_brown_county_czech.title: The Hrenchir Family and Other Moravians of Brown County, Kansas
key_book_for_brown_county_czech.author: Joan M. Hrenchir
key_book_for_brown_county_czech.relevance: Documents Sviadnov (Frýdek-Místek District) as origin of Brown County, Kansas Czech Catholic immigrants. The Hrenchir family settled in Everest, Kansas — same town and church as Frank Lepik's 1885 marriage. This book may contain surname indexes referencing Lepik.

archive_roadmap.action: Order Frank Lepick 1939 Kansas death certificate
archive_roadmap.address: 1000 SW Jackson St., Suite 120, Topeka, KS 66612
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: Frank's parents' names and exact village/town of birth in Czech lands. Kansas held death records from July 1, 1911. Frank died in 1939 — the certificate will exist. Whether the informant knew the parents' names and village depends on who reported the death (likely a child who may not have known specific Bohemian village).
archive_roadmap.access: Mail request or online via KDHE; also check if indexed on Ancestry or FamilySearch Kansas Death Records 1911-1944

archive_roadmap.action: Verify Geni profile 'František Lepík I' (parents: Cyril Lepík + Rosina Lepíková)
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: If this Geni profile is documented with Czech parish sources and covers the correct birth period (~1860s) in the Frýdek-Místek area, it could name Frank's parents or close relatives. Must be verified against primary Czech church records — Geni trees are user-contributed.
archive_roadmap.caution: Geni collaborative trees often contain unverified entries. Do not accept without Czech parish record confirmation.

archive_roadmap.action: Search Brown County, Kansas naturalization records 1881–1906
archive_roadmap.institution: Kansas State Historical Society (KSHS), 6425 SW 6th Ave., Topeka, KS 66615; also FamilySearch collection 4233290
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: Pre-1906 Declaration of Intent often records last place of residence in home country (names the village). FamilySearch has index to Kansas naturalizations 1856–1897. Frank Lepik does NOT appear in the Topeka Division federal naturalization index — suggests he naturalized through Brown County District Court (county-level naturalization was common pre-1906).
archive_roadmap.fee: Free via FamilySearch; KSHS in-person or mail request

archive_roadmap.action: Obtain and read Joan Hrenchir book on Moravians in Brown County Kansas (1997)
archive_roadmap.institution: CGSI Research Collection (Minnesota Genealogy Center, St. Paul, MN); also WorldCat (library loan)
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: This 1997 book specifically covers Sviadnov, Moravia immigrants who settled in Brown County, Kansas — the same community and Catholic church as Frank Lepik. The book may contain a surname index that includes Lepik, Mikeska, and neighboring families, potentially naming Frank's village.

archive_roadmap.action: Search Hamburg emigration lists 1880–1882 for Lepik/Lepick/Lepický variants
archive_roadmap.institution: Ancestry.com — Germany, Hamburg Emigration Lists collection (paid subscription required)
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: Ship manifest would name departure port (Hamburg or Bremen), ship name, destination, and often village of last residence in Europe. This is how many Czech immigrants are traced to their specific village.

archive_roadmap.action: Browse FamilySearch Opava Archive Church Books — Frýdek-Místek area parishes
archive_roadmap.collection: Czech Republic, Northern Moravia, Opava Archive Church Books, 1571–1905
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: Browse (not searchable by index) for Lepík baptisms in Frýdek-Místek area parishes 1855–1870 (Frank's birth record) and 1820–1845 (Frank's father's birth record). Parishes to browse: Místek (records from ~1630), Frýdek (~1630), Sviadnov (records under Místek parish), Frýdlant nad Ostravicí (1695), Jablunkov (1617).
archive_roadmap.limitation: Browse-only — no surname index. Must scroll through page images. Records written in German and Latin for this period.

archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: The Regional Archives in Opava has digitized Frýdek-Místek area parish records. The site requires working through a browser verification (Radware security) but is accessible in person or via Czech genealogy researcher.
archive_roadmap.alternative: Contact SOkA Frýdek-Místek (District Archive): https://www.archives.cz/web/soka/frydek-mistek/badatelna/ — offers study room access for in-person research

archive_roadmap.action: Contact Texas Czech Genealogical Society about Jaromir Lepik family
archive_roadmap.what_it_proves: The TCGS may have records for the Jaromir Lepik family in Moravia/Wharton County Texas. If Jaromir Lepik's Czech village of origin is known (via Texas Czech genealogy researchers), it would likely be the SAME village as Frank Lepik's family — given the surname's extreme rarity (88 Czech bearers total).

corrections.correction: Prior research files noted Frank Lepik's birth year as ~1862 based on census data; WikiTree Lepik-8 was fetched in this research pass and confirms birth year ~1862 (died 1939, age ~77). No correction needed.

Prior research stated Frank Lepik 'does NOT appear in the Topeka Division, District of Kansas naturalization declarations index (National Archives).' This research pass confirms this gap persists and that pre-1906 county-level naturalization (Brown County District Court) is the more likely avenue.

The Geni profiles for 'František Lepík I' (parents: Cyril + Rosina) and 'František Lepík (1888-1888)' (parents: Tomáš + Terezie) were identified as new leads in this research pass but could NOT be confirmed because the Geni pages returned no extractable content during WebFetch attempts. These parents' names are surface-indexed by search engines but must be treated as UNVERIFIED until confirmed against Czech parish records.
